content:
14 min read
3 hours ago

--

Most utility folders do not become messy because developers are careless. They become messy because nobody decides what belongs there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every codebase has a drawer.
Sometimes it is called utils.
Sometimes helpers.
Sometimes common.
Sometimes shared.
At first, it feels harmless. A date formatter goes there. A string normalizer goes there. A small array helper goes there. Nobody wants to overthink it, and nobody should. Not every function needs architecture. Not every reusable line deserves a folder strategy.
Then the project grows.
A permission check lands in utils. A billing calculation lands there. A payload transformer gets added because two screens need it. A feature flag helper appears. A function that touches local storage gets mixed with pure formatters. A few constants show up. Then API response mappers. Then validation rules. Then a strange function with five optional parameters that nobody wants to touch.
The folder still looks innocent.
But now it is not a utility folder.
It is a junk drawer for decisions the codebase did not know where to put.
The hidden problem is not that utility folders exist. They are useful when they hold truly general, stable, low-risk helpers. The problem is that many teams use them as an escape hatch for unclear ownership. When a function does not obviously belong to a feature, domain, service, or component, it gets dropped into utils because that feels neutral.
Neutral code is rarely neutral for long.
A utility folder becomes dangerous when it starts hiding business rules, side effects, policy decisions, and shared assumptions behind the most harmless name in the project.

The Folder Started Clean Because the Decisions Were Small
Most junk drawers start with reasonable choices.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A developer writes a date formatter. It is used in three places, so extracting it makes sense. Another developer adds capitalize. Someone else adds formatCurrency. The folder feels helpful because the functions are small, stable, and boring.
This is the good version of a utility folder. It contains code that does not care about the product. A function that trims a string, formats a number, safely parses JSON, or checks whether a value is empty can often live there without creating much risk. The function is not secretly deciding whether a user can access a feature. It is not shaping an API contract. It is not mutating global state. It is just doing a small technical job.
The trouble begins when the same folder becomes the answer to every placement question.
A developer does not know where to put canUserEditProject, so it goes into utils. Another does not know where to put buildInvoicePayload, so it goes there too. Someone needs getCurrentWorkspace, and because it is used in multiple places, it joins the same folder. The folder now contains formatting, permissions, billing, authentication assumptions, API shape decisions, and local storage behavior.
The codebase did not become messy because one person made a terrible decision. It drifted because each individual decision felt small.
That is why utility folders are so deceptive. They hide accumulation. Every new helper feels harmless in isolation. Nobody opens a pull request thinking, “Today I will weaken our architecture.” They think, “This is reused in two places, so I’ll put it in shared.”
The failure mode appears later when developers stop knowing what kind of code lives there. Is this folder for pure helpers? Domain logic? API mappers? Browser storage? Feature flags? Validation rules? Permission checks? Everything is technically reusable, so everything seems eligible.
That is when utils stops being a tool.
It becomes a place where the team postpones design.
Reuse Is Not Enough Reason to Make Something Shared
One of the most common mistakes around utility folders is confusing reuse with shared meaning.
Two places using similar code does not automatically mean the code deserves to be shared. It might mean the two places currently look similar but will change for different reasons. This difference matters more than most developers realize.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A date formatting function used across the app is probably safe to share. A billing rule used by checkout and admin reporting might not be. A payload mapper used by user creation and user update might look reusable today, but those flows may split the moment product rules change.
The problem is that utils makes sharing feel cheap. Move the function, import it everywhere, reduce duplication, and the diff looks cleaner. Code review often rewards this. Nobody likes repeated code. A shared helper feels mature because it appears to reduce waste.
But shared code creates a contract, even when nobody writes the contract down.
Once ten callers depend on a utility function, changing it is no longer a local decision. The function now has a blast radius. A small change for one screen can break another screen that depended on the old behavior. A new optional flag can preserve backward compatibility but make the function harder to reason about. A “quick fix” inside a shared helper can silently change behavior across the product.
Consider a function like this:
function buildUserPayload(user, includeMeta = false, fromAdmin = false) {
return {
id: user.id,
email: user.email?.trim().toLowerCase(),
role: fromAdmin ? user.role : user.publicRole,
meta: includeMeta ? user.meta : undefined
};
}This does not look terrible at first. It is short. It avoids duplication. It gives callers a few options.
But the flags are a signal. The function is serving multiple contexts that do not fully agree. The admin page, public profile page, onboarding flow, and API sync job may all need a user payload, but not the same one. The shared helper hides that difference behind parameters.
Soon nobody knows which combination is valid. A developer passes true, false because another caller did. Someone else flips a flag to fix one bug and creates another. Tests cover the function, but not every real context. The function is reused, but the meaning is unstable.
Reuse is valuable when the underlying concept is stable.
When the concept is still changing, premature sharing can create coupling disguised as cleanliness.
The better question is not, “Can this be reused?”
The better question is, “Should these callers change together?”
If the answer is no, your utility folder may be collecting future bugs.
Domain Logic Does Not Become Generic Because You Put It in Utils
A function does not become general just because it lives in a general folder.
This is one of the quietest architecture mistakes in codebases. Teams move domain logic into utils and accidentally make it look less important than it is. A permission rule becomes a helper. A pricing calculation becomes a utility. A workflow decision becomes a common function. The location makes the code feel technical, but the behavior is product logic.
That mismatch creates confusion.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A function called canAccessDashboard is not just a helper if it depends on plan type, role, workspace status, feature flags, trial expiration, and internal admin override. That is authorization policy. It belongs somewhere with enough context to make its importance clear.
When domain rules live in generic folders, they become easier to call and harder to govern. Developers import them without understanding the business meaning. Reviewers treat changes as implementation details. Tests focus on inputs and outputs, but not the product scenarios behind them. Over time, the codebase loses the ability to see which functions represent actual business decisions.
The failure mode is subtle but expensive. Two teams start using the same helper for different meanings of “access.” One means the user can open the page. Another means the user can perform an action. Another means the user can see a disabled button. The helper returns a boolean, so everyone feels safe. Then a bug appears where users can see something they should not modify, or cannot access something they paid for.
The code did not fail because a utility function existed.
It failed because the utility folder erased context.
Domain logic needs a home that matches its meaning. Permissions should live near permissions. Billing logic should live near billing. API contract mapping should live near the API boundary. Feature-specific rules should live near the feature until they are stable enough to extract.
This does not mean every project needs heavy architecture. It means important decisions should not be hidden in the least meaningful folder name available.
A folder named utils tells the reader, “Nothing special here.”
That is a dangerous lie when the function decides real product behavior.
Side Effects Hide Too Easily in Helper Folders
A utility function should usually be boring to call.
The reader should not have to wonder whether it writes to local storage, changes global state, sends analytics, refreshes a token, clears a cache, or redirects the user. If a function does those things, the name and location should make the cost visible.
Utility folders often fail this test.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

They begin with pure helpers, then gradually collect functions that interact with the outside world. getSession reads from storage and refreshes expired tokens. trackEvent wraps analytics. syncUser updates state and writes to cache. getWorkspace reads from local storage and falls back to an API value. All of them live beside formatDate and capitalize.
This mixture damages trust.
When pure functions and side-effecting functions live in the same place, developers lose the ability to reason by location. Importing from utils no longer tells them anything about safety. A function might be harmless, or it might trigger behavior somewhere else in the app.
This matters during debugging. A component rerenders unexpectedly. A cached value changes. A user gets logged out after opening a page. An API request happens twice. The team looks at the component, the network tab, the state store, and the backend. Eventually, someone finds a helper that looked like a read but performed a write.
That kind of bug feels exhausting because the code violated expectation.
The function did not look dangerous enough to be suspected.
This is especially painful in frontend applications where state already lives across many places. URL parameters, React state, server cache, Redux, local storage, cookies, and derived values can all be involved. A utility helper that silently connects two of those systems can create a debugging path that is much longer than the function itself.
The better approach is not ceremony for everything. It is honesty.
Pure helpers can live together. Side-effecting operations should be named and placed in a way that makes their behavior obvious. A function that stores, syncs, tracks, refreshes, clears, redirects, mutates, or fetches should not pretend to be a harmless utility.
Good structure reduces surprise.
A junk drawer creates it.
The Utils Folder Becomes a Map of Avoided Ownership
When a codebase has a huge utility folder, the problem is often not the folder itself.
The problem is ownership.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

utils grows when the team does not know where decisions belong. Is this function part of the feature? The domain? The API layer? The UI layer? The state layer? The shared platform? When nobody wants to answer that question, the utility folder becomes the easiest compromise.
That compromise feels efficient at first. The developer avoids a debate. The pull request stays small. The code ships. But the codebase pays later because each avoided decision becomes another ambiguous function.
A large utility folder often tells a story.
It shows where the domain model is weak. It shows which produc
